title: The Elevator Footage That Terrified the Internet
description:
The Case of Elisa Lam (2013) — On January 31, 2013, Canadian tourist Elisa Lam disappeared from the Cecil Hotel in downtown Los Angeles. Nineteen days later, her body was found in a water tank on the hotel's roof after guests complained about the water. Disturbing elevator surveillance footage of her final moments went viral, sparking worldwide debate.

Sources: Los Angeles Police Department case files, Los Angeles County Department of Medical Examiner-Coroner autopsy report, Cecil Hotel incident records, LAPD press releases.

DISCLAIMER: This video is for educational and documentary purposes only. All information presented is sourced from publicly available records, news reports, and official statements. Viewer discretion is advised.

#truecrime #elisalam #cecilhotel #mystery #losangeles #elevator #unsolved #documentary #hotel #darkfiles
sources: Los Angeles Police Department official case files
Los Angeles County Medical Examiner-Coroner autopsy report
Cecil Hotel incident records and guest logs
Netflix documentary 'Crime Scene: The Vanishing at the Cecil Hotel' (2021)

segments.voice: In the heart of downtown Los Angeles stands a building with a history so dark it seems almost fictional. The Cecil Hotel, built in 1927, has been the site of at least sixteen deaths, multiple serial killer residents, and countless unsolved incidents over its nearly century-long existence. In January 2013, a twenty-one-year-old Canadian tourist named Elisa Lam checked into the Cecil Hotel alone. She would never check out. Her case would become one of the most discussed mysteries of the internet age.
segments.visual: old hotel building exterior downtown city

segments.voice: Elisa Lam was a student at the University of British Columbia in Vancouver, Canada. According to her family and friends, she was bright, curious, and passionate about travel. She maintained an active blog on Tumblr where she documented her thoughts, her struggles with mental health, and her experiences. In January 2013, she embarked on a solo trip down the West Coast, visiting San Diego and other California cities before arriving in Los Angeles. She chose the Cecil Hotel for its affordable rates, unaware of its horrifying reputation.
segments.visual: young woman with travel backpack exploring city

segments.voice: The Cecil Hotel has earned the nickname 'Hotel Death' over its long and troubled history. According to Los Angeles city records and historical archives, the hotel has been connected to two of the most notorious serial killers in American history. Richard Ramirez, the Night Stalker, lived at the Cecil during his killing spree in 1985. Jack Unterweger, an Austrian serial killer, stayed there while murdering women in 1991. The hotel's history of violence and tragedy stretches back to the 1930s and includes suicides, murders, and mysterious deaths.
segments.visual: vintage hotel hallway long corridor dimly lit

segments.voice: Elisa checked into the Cecil Hotel on January 26, 2013. According to hotel records obtained by police, she was initially placed in a shared room on one of the upper floors. However, her roommates complained about her behavior, describing it as strange and erratic. She was subsequently moved to a private room. According to the LAPD investigation, Elisa was last seen alive on January 31st by hotel staff in the lobby area. After that date, she simply vanished from all records and surveillance within the building.
segments.visual: hotel lobby vintage interior ornate

segments.voice: When Elisa failed to check out or contact her family, her parents in Vancouver grew alarmed and called the Los Angeles Police Department. According to LAPD records, a missing persons investigation was opened on February 6, 2013. Detectives searched the Cecil Hotel from top to bottom, including the roof, which housed four large water cisterns that supplied the building's water system. However, according to the official report, the rooftop search did not include looking inside the water tanks. This would prove to be a critical oversight.
segments.visual: industrial rooftop water tanks metal

segments.voice: On February 13, 2013, the LAPD released a piece of surveillance footage that would become one of the most watched and debated videos in internet history. The footage, captured by a camera in one of the Cecil Hotel's elevators, showed Elisa Lam in the elevator on the night she disappeared. According to LAPD spokesperson Officer Sara Faden, the footage was released to the public in hopes that someone might recognize Elisa and provide information about her whereabouts. No one was prepared for what the video showed.
segments.visual: elevator interior security camera angle

segments.voice: The elevator footage shows Elisa entering the elevator and pressing multiple floor buttons. She then appears to wait for the elevator doors to close, but they remain open. She steps out into the hallway, looks in both directions as if checking for someone, then retreats back into the elevator. Her movements become increasingly agitated and bizarre. She appears to be talking to someone who is not visible on camera. She makes strange hand gestures, waving her arms in unusual patterns. The doors remain open throughout the entire sequence.
segments.visual: empty elevator doors opening and closing

segments.voice: The footage continues for approximately four minutes. According to the timestamp on the surveillance video, the incident occurred on January 31, 2013. Elisa eventually steps out of the elevator and moves to the left, out of camera view. After she leaves, the elevator doors finally close and the car begins moving through floors normally. The video was edited by the LAPD before release, with a portion slowed down. The department stated this was done for clarity purposes, though conspiracy theorists would later question whether portions had been deliberately removed.
segments.visual: surveillance monitor showing multiple camera feeds

segments.voice: The elevator video went viral within hours of its release, garnering millions of views across YouTube and social media platforms. According to media analytics, it became one of the most discussed pieces of surveillance footage in internet history. Viewers were deeply disturbed by Elisa's behavior. Theories proliferated wildly. Some believed she was being pursued by an unseen assailant. Others suggested she was experiencing a psychotic episode. Some even proposed supernatural explanations, pointing to the Cecil Hotel's dark history as evidence of paranormal activity.
segments.visual: computer screen showing viral video trending

segments.voice: Nineteen days after Elisa was last seen alive, guests at the Cecil Hotel began complaining about the water. According to maintenance reports and guest complaints filed with the hotel, the water pressure had been unusually low, and the water itself had a strange taste and a dark, unusual color. Some guests reported the water had an odd smell. On February 19, 2013, a maintenance worker was sent to the rooftop to inspect the hotel's four water cisterns. What he found inside one of the tanks would horrify the nation.
segments.visual: water running from faucet dark colored

segments.voice: According to the Los Angeles Police Department report, the maintenance worker discovered the body of Elisa Lam floating in one of the hotel's rooftop water tanks. She was unclothed, with her personal belongings, including her clothing and identification, floating in the water beside her. The water tank was approximately eight feet tall and four feet in diameter, accessible only by a ladder on the side. The tank's heavy lid was reportedly open when the body was found, though conflicting reports would later emerge about whether it was fully or partially open.
segments.visual: industrial water storage tank on building roof

segments.voice: The discovery raised immediate and deeply troubling questions. According to building records, access to the Cecil Hotel's rooftop was restricted. The roof was accessible only through a locked door that triggered an alarm when opened, or through a fire escape. According to the LAPD investigation, there was no record of the roof alarm being triggered around the time of Elisa's disappearance. How did she reach the roof? How did she get into the water tank? And if someone else was involved, how did they bypass the building's security systems?
segments.visual: locked door with alarm system on building

segments.voice: The Los Angeles County Department of Medical Examiner-Coroner conducted an autopsy on Elisa Lam's body. According to the official autopsy report, released months later, the cause of death was determined to be accidental drowning. The manner of death was classified as accident. The report noted that bipolar disorder was listed as a significant contributing condition. Toxicology results showed that Elisa had therapeutic levels of her prescribed medications in her system, including mood stabilizers and antidepressants. No alcohol or illegal drugs were detected.
segments.visual: medical examiner office building exterior

segments.voice: The official ruling of accidental death was met with widespread skepticism. According to critics and online investigators, several aspects of the case did not add up. How could a woman accidentally climb into a water tank that required navigating to a restricted rooftop, climbing a ladder, opening a heavy lid, and then entering the water? The tanks were not easy to access and would have required deliberate effort to enter. Some experts argued the accidental drowning ruling was premature and that the investigation had not adequately explored all possibilities.
segments.visual: question mark on dark background mysterious

segments.voice: Elisa's mental health history became a central element of the investigation. According to the coroner's report and her family's statements, Elisa had been diagnosed with bipolar disorder and depression. She was prescribed multiple medications to manage her conditions. Her Tumblr blog contained candid discussions about her struggles with mental illness, including episodes of mania and depression. Some experts suggested that the elevator footage showed behavior consistent with a manic or psychotic episode, which could explain her erratic movements and apparent conversations with no one.
segments.visual: journal diary pages handwritten thoughts

segments.voice: According to psychiatric experts consulted during the investigation, individuals experiencing acute manic episodes can exhibit exactly the type of behavior seen in the elevator footage. Pressured speech, grandiose or paranoid thinking, impaired judgment, and erratic physical movements are all consistent with mania. If Elisa was in the midst of such an episode, she may have accessed the rooftop through impulsive exploration and entered the water tank without understanding the danger. The open lid could have closed behind her, trapping her inside with no way to climb out.
segments.visual: hospital corridor empty clinical lighting

segments.voice: However, the mental health explanation does not satisfy everyone. According to independent investigators and journalists who examined the case, the physical challenges of reaching the water tank remain difficult to explain. The rooftop was accessible only through alarmed doors or an exterior fire escape. The water tanks were elevated on platforms and required climbing a ladder. The lids were heavy. Some investigators questioned whether a young woman in the grip of a psychological crisis could have navigated all these obstacles alone and in the dark of night.
segments.visual: fire escape metal stairs on building exterior

segments.voice: The Cecil Hotel's own security records added confusion to the investigation. According to documents obtained through the investigation, the hotel's surveillance system had significant gaps in coverage. Not all hallways were monitored, and the rooftop and fire escape areas had no cameras. The elevator footage was the last visual record of Elisa alive. According to LAPD investigators, they reviewed hundreds of hours of additional surveillance footage from the hotel but found no other images of Elisa after the elevator video. She had simply vanished from the camera network.
segments.visual: security camera mounted on ceiling corner

segments.voice: The internet's response to the case was unprecedented in its scope and intensity. According to media analysts, the case spawned thousands of Reddit threads, YouTube analysis videos, blog posts, and social media discussions. Amateur detectives analyzed every frame of the elevator footage, scrutinized the hotel's history, and proposed elaborate theories involving murder, government conspiracies, and paranormal activity. Some online sleuths even harassed innocent people, falsely identifying them as suspects based on flimsy circumstantial connections. The case became a cautionary tale about internet vigilantism.
segments.visual: social media feeds scrolling on screen

segments.voice: One internet theory centered on a disturbing coincidence. According to bloggers and online researchers, a tuberculosis test called LAM-ELISA was being used in the skid row area near the Cecil Hotel at approximately the same time as Elisa's disappearance. The apparent connection between the test's name and the victim's name fueled conspiracy theories about bioterrorism and government experiments. Medical authorities and investigators dismissed these theories as pure coincidence, but the connection continued to circulate online for years, feeding the case's growing mythology.
segments.visual: laboratory test tubes scientific research

segments.voice: A musician named Morbid was falsely accused of involvement in Elisa's death by internet investigators. According to news reports, the man, a Mexican death metal artist who had stayed at the Cecil Hotel months before Elisa's visit, was subjected to intense online harassment after amateur sleuths connected him to the case based solely on his dark musical persona and his history of staying at the hotel. He was eventually cleared by police, who confirmed he was not even in the country at the time. The false accusations nearly destroyed his life.
segments.visual: person alone at desk head in hands stressed

segments.voice: In 2021, Netflix released a four-part documentary series titled 'Crime Scene: The Vanishing at the Cecil Hotel.' According to the filmmakers, the series aimed to examine both Elisa's case and the broader history of the Cecil Hotel within the context of downtown Los Angeles. The documentary featured interviews with hotel staff, investigators, and journalists, and provided a comprehensive look at the building's troubled past. It also addressed the role of internet culture in shaping public perception of the case, including the harm caused by online speculation.
segments.visual: documentary film crew with lighting equipment

segments.voice: The documentary revealed new details about the hotel's security lapses. According to former Cecil Hotel employees interviewed in the series, the roof alarm was frequently disabled because it was triggered too often by hotel guests trying to access the roof for views of the city. This revelation significantly undermined one of the central mysteries of the case, how Elisa reached the rooftop. If the alarm was routinely turned off, she could have simply walked through the door without detection, particularly during the late-night hours when staff was minimal.
segments.visual: city skyline view from rooftop at night

segments.voice: According to the Los Angeles County Medical Examiner-Coroner's final report, the investigation took into account Elisa's documented history of bipolar disorder, the evidence of her medications in her bloodstream, the behavior captured in the elevator footage, and the physical evidence at the scene. The report concluded there was no evidence of foul play, no evidence of trauma beyond drowning, and no evidence of assault. The official determination remained accidental drowning with bipolar disorder as a significant condition. The case was closed.
segments.visual: official document stamp seal close up

segments.voice: Elisa Lam's family has sought to preserve her memory beyond the circumstances of her death. According to media reports, her parents established a foundation in her name and have asked the public to remember Elisa for who she was, a curious, thoughtful young woman who loved travel and literature, rather than as a mystery to be solved. Her Tumblr blog, which was set to automatically post scheduled entries even after her death, continued publishing for months, creating an eerie digital afterlife that added another layer of poignancy to an already heartbreaking story.
segments.visual: memorial candles and flowers tribute

segments.voice: The Cecil Hotel itself has undergone a transformation in the years since Elisa's death. According to Los Angeles city records, the building was renovated and partially rebranded as Stay on Main, an attempt to distance the property from its dark reputation. However, the Cecil Hotel name persists in public consciousness. Dark tourism has turned the building into a macabre attraction, with visitors photographing the exterior and the rooftop water tanks. The hotel has become inseparable from the tragedies that occurred within its walls.
segments.visual: hotel renovation construction work building

segments.voice: According to mental health professionals who have commented on the case, Elisa's story highlights the vulnerability of individuals with mental illness, particularly when traveling alone. Bipolar disorder can cause episodes that are unpredictable and dangerous, especially if medication routines are disrupted by travel. Some experts have suggested that the time zone changes, irregular sleep, and unfamiliar environment of solo travel may have triggered the episode that led to Elisa's death. Her case has become a catalyst for discussions about mental health awareness and travel safety.
segments.visual: sunrise over city buildings new day

segments.voice: The water contamination aspect of the case remains deeply disturbing. According to Los Angeles County health department records, the Cecil Hotel's water supply was potentially contaminated by Elisa's remains for up to nineteen days before her body was discovered. During that time, hundreds of hotel guests used the water for drinking, bathing, and cooking. Health officials conducted tests and stated there was no significant health risk, but the psychological impact on guests who learned the truth was profound. Several guests later sued the hotel.
segments.visual: water glass on table close up clear

segments.voice: Elisa Lam's case exists at the intersection of real tragedy and internet mythology. According to scholars who have studied the case's cultural impact, it represents a new type of true crime phenomenon, one where the line between legitimate investigation and entertainment becomes dangerously blurred. The viral elevator footage transformed a young woman's death into content, consumed and analyzed by millions who never knew her. Her story is a mirror reflecting our own fascination with darkness and our sometimes reckless pursuit of answers at the expense of human dignity.
segments.visual: mirror reflection dark hallway mysterious

segments.voice: Today, the elevator footage continues to circulate online, still generating new theories and discussions years after the case was officially closed. According to the LAPD, no new evidence has emerged that would warrant reopening the investigation. But for millions of viewers who have watched Elisa Lam's final recorded moments, the footage remains haunting. A young woman, alone in an elevator, seemingly frightened of something unseen, making gestures that defy easy explanation. Then she steps out of frame, and the doors finally close. Whatever happened next, only the Cecil Hotel knows.
segments.visual: elevator doors closing in empty hallway
